planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in moist, strongly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; hoary blueberry grows naturally at higher elevations in the southern Appalachians in moist acidic forest understory and open heath balds.

directions: Plant in moist, strongly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; hoary blueberry is a native Appalachian species of high-elevation heath and forest understory communities, distinguished by its distinctly hairy (hoary) twigs and leaf undersides — the fine hairs give new growth and undersides a grayish, woolly appearance. The plant is closely related to rabbiteye blueberry but adapted to cooler, higher-elevation sites. The blue-black berries are edible and well-flavored. In the garden, it performs well in moist, acidic shade conditions that suit other ericaceous plants like rhododendron and mountain laurel. An excellent native for the acid woodland garden in zones 5–8.

planting.note: Divide rhizomatous colonies in spring or plant container stock in strongly acidic, sandy, well-drained to dry soil in full sun to partial shade; dwarf huckleberry is native to the dry, sandy, acidic pine flatwoods and coastal plain scrub of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, strongly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; dwarf huckleberry is a low-growing, colony-forming native shrub of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain longleaf pine ecosystem, spreading by rhizomes across the forest floor of dry, sandy pine flatwoods and scrub habitats. The small, black to deep blue-black berries are edible and flavorful — similar in taste to other huckleberries but produced on a very low-growing plant. The sticky, gland-dotted leaves are a distinctive feature. In the garden, it makes an excellent low, spreading ground cover for dry, sandy, sunny, acidic sites in zone 9a where few plants thrive. It is fire-adapted and rebounds strongly after prescribed burns in its natural habitat. An underused native ground cover for longleaf pine ecosystem restoration and dry, acidic, native plant gardens.

planting.note: Plant tip-rooted cuttings in fall or early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil in full sun; southern dewberry is a trailing species that tips-root naturally wherever the cane touches soil. It grows in dry, sandy, disturbed areas, road banks, and open pine flatwoods of the Southeast.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile to lean soil in full sun; southern dewberry is a trailing or low-arching native bramble of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain, producing large, juicy, black berries in late spring that are among the most flavorful of any native Rubus. The berries are excellent for eating fresh, preserving, and making wine and cobblers. The trailing canes root where they touch the soil, naturally colonizing disturbed, sandy, open habitats. Unlike upright blackberries, the low, trailing canes of dewberry are less showy but provide excellent wildlife value — the berries are consumed by birds, small mammals, and box turtles. In zone 9a, dewberry is one of the first fruits to ripen in spring (typically April–May), well before blackberries and blueberries.

planting.note: Plant tip-rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; bristly dewberry is adapted to moist, shaded habitats — wetland margins, bog edges, and moist forest floors — unlike most blackberries that prefer dry, open conditions.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; bristly dewberry is a trailing native bramble of moist, shaded, acidic habitats — sphagnum bog margins, moist pine flatwoods, wet woodland edges, and streambanks — producing small, rounded, black berries with a pleasant, mildly tart flavor. The semi-evergreen leaves have a distinctive leathery texture and glossy appearance. The plant is armed with tiny, bristle-like prickles (hence 'bristly') rather than the stout thorns of most blackberries. An important understory plant for moist, shaded, acidic native gardens where it provides both wildlife fruit value and ground cover function. Thrives in conditions too wet and shaded for most other fruiting plants.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp fly honeysuckle grows naturally in boggy, moist, shrubby areas and at the margins of fens, bogs, and wet meadows across the northern US and Canada.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp fly honeysuckle is a native shrub honeysuckle of northern boggy habitats — a deciduous shrub with paired, yellowish-white flowers in late spring and attractive paired red berries in summer. Unlike the invasive Japanese honeysuckle vine (Lonicera japonica), this is a well-behaved native shrub that does not spread aggressively. The berries, while not favored as human food, are consumed by numerous bird species in summer and early fall. The shrub provides nesting structure for a variety of native birds in boggy habitats. An excellent native choice for consistently moist to wet, acidic, partially shaded sites in the cold-hardy northern zones 2–6.
